statement: Obsidian is a local-first, Markdown-based knowledge management app. Its plugin API wraps CodeMirror 6 (editor), Electron (desktop runtime), and a custom Vault abstraction (file system). Plugins run in a Node.js-like environment with filesystem access (desktop only). Core philosophy: Obsidian owns the Vault (folder of .md files), plugins are guests. UNSAID: mobile plugins have a restricted API — no Node.js built-ins, no native modules. Desktop plugins that use require('fs') directly will fail on mobile.

Logseq is an outliner-based PKM tool with a graph database (Datascript) under the hood. Unlike Obsidian (tree of markdown files), Logseq's data model is a flat graph of blocks with parent/child relationships. Plugins use the logseq.Editor API and Datascript queries for data access. UNSAID: Logseq is transitioning to a new DB version (Logseq DB) that breaks backward compatibility with existing plugins. Plugins for classic Logseq may not work with DB version.

Obsidian plugins extend Plugin class with onload() and onunload(). Resources registered in onload() MUST be released in onunload() to prevent memory leaks. this.registerEvent(), this.addCommand(), this.addRibbonIcon() auto-unregister on unload. UNSAID: raw addEventListener() calls on DOM elements or app.workspace.on() WITHOUT using this.registerEvent() will leak — the event handler persists after the plugin is disabled/reloaded. This is the most common source of ghost behavior after plugin updates.

Obsidian's Vault API (this.app.vault) is the ONLY correct way to read/write files. Direct Node.js fs operations bypass Obsidian's cache and event system — changes won't trigger vault.on('modify') events and the metadata cache won't update. Key methods: vault.read(), vault.create(), vault.modify(), vault.rename(), vault.delete(). For metadata (frontmatter, links), use MetadataCache: app.metadataCache.getFileCache(). Extraction signal: plugins using require('fs') for vault files are doing it wrong.

Obsidian plugin settings use loadData()/saveData() for persistence (stored in .obsidian/plugins/<id>/data.json). The PluginSettingTab class provides the settings UI panel. UNSAID: settings are stored as plain JSON — no schema migration built in. When plugin updates add new settings keys, old users' data.json won't have them. Best practice: use Object.assign({}, DEFAULT_SETTINGS, await this.loadData()) to merge defaults with saved settings, ensuring new keys get their defaults.

Obsidian's editor is CodeMirror 6. Custom decorations, syntax highlighting, and inline widgets use CodeMirror 6's extension system (ViewPlugin, StateField, Decoration). UNSAID: CodeMirror 6 is a reactive system — extensions react to state transactions. Performing DOM manipulation directly on editor elements (not via CM6 decorations) will be wiped on the next CM6 render cycle. Extraction signal: plugins that modify .cm-editor DOM directly are fragile; correct plugins use ViewPlugin with decorations.

Obsidian Dataview plugin enables SQL-like queries over vault metadata. Basic TABLE query: `TABLE file.mtime, status FROM #project WHERE status != "done" SORT file.mtime DESC`. Inline queries: `= this.status` renders the current file's frontmatter value inline. UNSAID: Dataview reads frontmatter (YAML between `---`) and inline fields (`key:: value` syntax). Dataview is read-only — it cannot modify files. Performance degrades with large vaults (>5000 notes) because Dataview indexes the entire vault in memory. The `dataviewjs` query type gives full JavaScript access for complex aggregations but requires understanding Dataview's async API (`dv.pages()`, `dv.table()`). Dataview queries are live — they re-render on vault changes, which causes visible flickering in large dashboards.

Obsidian sync conflict resolution: when the same note is edited on two devices before sync, Obsidian Sync creates a conflict copy (`Note (conflict 1).md`). UNSAID: conflict copies are silent — there's no notification in the UI, only a new file in the vault. Without a periodic conflict review process, conflicted notes accumulate unresolved. The Obsidian Sync conflict strategy is last-write-wins at the paragraph level (not file level) — but this only works when both devices are online and syncing regularly. Offline editing on mobile + desktop creates the highest conflict rate. Workaround: use a Dataview query to surface all `(conflict)` files: `LIST FROM "" WHERE contains(file.name, "conflict")`. iCloud sync + Obsidian (without Obsidian Sync) is a common source of data corruption — iCloud's sync timing and .obsidian/ directory conflicts with plugin state files.

Obsidian graph view visualizes wikilink connections between notes. The local graph (per-note) shows connections up to a specified depth. Useful for: discovering orphan notes, finding unexpected connections, validating MOC (Map of Content) structure. UNSAID: the global graph view is visually impressive but cognitively overwhelming for vaults >200 notes — most users find the local graph more actionable. Graph analysis insight: high-degree nodes (many incoming links) identify hub concepts; isolated nodes indicate notes that need linking or archival. Obsidian's graph doesn't show Dataview virtual links — only explicit `[[wikilinks]]`. For programmatic graph analysis (clustering, centrality), export links via Dataview and process with Python/networkx outside Obsidian.

Obsidian Templater plugin (community) extends the built-in Templates core plugin with dynamic templates. Templater enables: `<% tp.date.now('YYYY-MM-DD') %>` for dates, `<% tp.file.title %>` for current file name, `<% await tp.system.prompt('Enter value') %>` for user input. Templates can run JavaScript functions. UNSAID: Templater's `tp.file.rename()` and `tp.file.move()` enable auto-organization — templates that create a file in a temp location then move it based on metadata. The `on_new_file_creation` trigger runs Templater automatically when a new file is created in a configured folder. Common mistake: using Templater syntax in the built-in Templates plugin (or vice versa) — the syntax is different (`{{title}}` vs `<% tp.file.title %>`).

Obsidian CSS snippets enable vault-wide visual customization. Snippets are CSS files placed in `.obsidian/snippets/` and enabled per-snippet in Settings → Appearance. CSS variables (--color-base-00 through --color-base-100, --font-text-size, etc.) are the correct override targets — theme-compatible and don't break on theme updates. UNSAID: direct element CSS targeting (`div.cm-line { }`) breaks when Obsidian updates CodeMirror internals — CSS variables are stable contracts. The Style Settings community plugin provides a UI for theme CSS variables without writing CSS. A common use case: callout styling — `[data-callout='warning'] { --callout-color: 220, 50, 50; }` customizes specific callout types. Mobile and desktop render the same CSS snippets — use `@media (max-width: 768px)` for device-specific overrides.

Logseq Datalog/Datascript queries give programmatic access to the graph database. Basic query: `{{query (and [[ProjectA]] (task TODO))}}` finds blocks tagged with ProjectA that have TODO status. Advanced queries use EDN format: `{:title "My Query" :query [:find (pull ?b [*]) :where [?b :block/marker "TODO"] [?b :block/refs [:block/name "projecta"]]]}`. UNSAID: Logseq queries operate on BLOCKS not pages — the unit is a bullet point, not a file. This is fundamentally different from Obsidian Dataview (which operates on files). Logseq's new DB version (2024+) changes the underlying schema — EDN queries written for the classic file-based version will need rewriting for DB version. The `:block/content` field contains the raw block text including Markdown syntax.

Community plugin selection criteria for Obsidian: prefer plugins with (1) >10k downloads, (2) active GitHub commits within 6 months, (3) compatible with current Obsidian API version (`minAppVersion` in manifest.json). The Obsidian plugin API changes across major versions — plugins using deprecated APIs show yellow warnings in Settings. UNSAID: installing many plugins has real performance costs — each plugin's `onload()` runs at startup, and active plugins consume memory. The BRAT (Beta Reviewer's Auto-update Tester) community plugin enables installing pre-release plugins from GitHub — useful for testing but risky for production vaults. Core plugins (bundled with Obsidian) are maintained by the Obsidian team and receive compatibility updates with each release — prefer core plugins over community alternatives when functionality overlaps.

Obsidian vs Logseq trade-off: Obsidian is file-first (Markdown files are primary, app is a viewer), Logseq is graph-first (database is primary, files are export). Obsidian's advantage: portability (open any .md file in any editor), ecosystem compatibility (git, Pandoc, static site generators). Logseq's advantage: outliner UX (daily notes + block references + bidirectional links are first-class), built-in task management, journaling workflow. UNSAID: Logseq's new DB version (2024+) abandons the file-first model entirely — data lives in a proprietary database, not Markdown files. This is a fundamental philosophical shift that makes Logseq DB less portable than classic Logseq. For teams requiring version-controlled, git-diffable notes, Obsidian remains the clear choice. For personal daily journaling with outliner workflow, Logseq (classic) remains superior.

Obsidian backup best practices: the `.obsidian/` folder contains all plugin data, settings, and workspace state — it must be included in backups. Git-based backup strategy: commit the entire vault including `.obsidian/` for full reproducibility. Use `.gitignore` to exclude: `.obsidian/workspace.json` (per-session state that changes on every open), `.obsidian/cache` (rebuild-able), large attachments. UNSAID: Obsidian Sync and Git sync running simultaneously on the same vault cause race conditions — Obsidian Sync changes file timestamps which triggers git dirty state. Use one or the other, not both. The Obsidian Git community plugin provides automatic commit+push on vault changes — configure `Vault backup interval` to 20+ minutes to avoid commit spam. For mobile backup without Obsidian Sync, iCloud (iOS) or Dropsync (Android) work but require careful .obsidian/ exclusion to avoid cross-platform plugin config conflicts.

Logseq plugin development pitfalls: Logseq's plugin SDK (`@logseq/libs`) wraps a postMessage-based IFrame communication channel — all plugin API calls are async even when they look synchronous. UNSAID: `logseq.Editor.insertBlock()` returns a Promise — missing the await causes race conditions where subsequent operations run before the block exists. The `logseq.DB.onChanged()` callback fires for EVERY graph change — expensive computations in this callback block the main thread and degrade editor responsiveness. Batch DB operations using `logseq.DB.datascriptQuery()` instead of iterating with `logseq.Editor.getBlock()` per item. Logseq plugin hot-reload requires `logseq.useSettingsSchema()` to be called in `main()` before any other API — plugins that call API methods before `logseq.ready()` resolves will fail silently on some Logseq versions.

Obsidian Canvas (core plugin) enables spatial note-taking — a freeform infinite canvas with note cards, images, and links. Canvas files use `.canvas` JSON format (listable in Vault API). UNSAID: Canvas is not indexed by search or Dataview — links FROM canvas to notes are tracked in the backlinks panel of the target note, but content ON canvas is not searchable. The Obsidian Canvas format is open (JSON), enabling programmatic canvas generation — useful for auto-generating architecture diagrams or concept maps from structured data. Canvas performance degrades with >200 nodes — use nested canvases (embed a canvas inside a canvas) for large diagrams. Cards on a canvas can embed notes as live previews, meaning edits to the underlying note immediately reflect on the canvas without manual refresh.

Obsidian publish workflow: Obsidian Publish is the official hosted option — selective publishing of notes with a custom domain. For self-hosted alternatives: Quartz (static site generator for Obsidian vaults) and Digital Garden plugin (deploys to Netlify/Vercel). Quartz converts wikilinks and Dataview queries to HTML. UNSAID: Obsidian Publish doesn't execute Dataview queries — they render as raw code blocks. Plugins that generate content (Dataview, Templater output) must be replaced with static content before publishing. The `publish: true` frontmatter flag in Obsidian Publish selectively includes notes — without this flag (or with `publish: false`), notes are private. A common mistake: publishing a note that embeds a private note via wikilink — Obsidian Publish breaks the link silently rather than publishing the private embedded note.
